tool_call.args_raw: {"path":"/tmp/wd_test_short.md","content":"Rust's ownership <wd:1>model is a <wd:2>cornerstone feature that <wd:3>guarantees memory safety <wd:4>without requiring garbage <wd:5>collection. When you <wd:6>write Rust programs, the <wd:7]compiler tracks every value's <wd:8]ownership and determines <wd:9]when variables are no <wd:10]longer needed so it <wd:11]can automatically clean <wd:12]up memory. This system <wd:13]prevents common bugs like <wd:14]dangling pointers and double <wd:15]free errors at compile <wd:16]time. Ownership consists <wd:17]of three key rules <wd:18]that govern how data <wd:19]moves through your program. First, each <wd:20]value has exactly one <wd:21]owner variable. Second, you <wd:22]can reassign ownership to <wd:23]move values between variables, <wd:24]making the old binding invalid. Third, when a variable's scope ends, Rust drops it safely. These rules enable fearless concurrency and memory-safe code without runtime overhead or complexity."}

args_json.content: Rust's ownership <wd:1>model is a <wd:2>cornerstone feature that <wd:3>guarantees memory safety <wd:4>without requiring garbage <wd:5>collection. When you <wd:6>write Rust programs, the <wd:7]compiler tracks every value's <wd:8]ownership and determines <wd:9]when variables are no <wd:10]longer needed so it <wd:11]can automatically clean <wd:12]up memory. This system <wd:13]prevents common bugs like <wd:14]dangling pointers and double <wd:15]free errors at compile <wd:16]time. Ownership consists <wd:17]of three key rules <wd:18]that govern how data <wd:19]moves through your program. First, each <wd:20]value has exactly one <wd:21]owner variable. Second, you <wd:22]can reassign ownership to <wd:23]move values between variables, <wd:24]making the old binding invalid. Third, when a variable's scope ends, Rust drops it safely. These rules enable fearless concurrency and memory-safe code without runtime overhead or complexity.
